narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 43% chance that you might fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction is based on your profile, including your solid 7th-decile rankings in both Year 1 and Year 3 grades—better than the typical student's 5th or 6th decile—along with a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 34, an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, being female, White, and planning full-time studies. While many applicants share similar demographics like being White or full-time, your LSAT and GPA fall a bit below the averages of 36.7 and 3.21 seen across past students. The model's decision was influenced most strongly by your Year 3 decile ranking, which nudged the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure despite being above average. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also pushing slightly toward that risk. Your middle family income followed closely, contributing in the same direction. Then came your LSAT score, your racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender, each playing a smaller but still risk-increasing role in the model's thinking. Overall, no single factor stood out overwhelmingly, but these elements together tipped the scales compared to profiles of students who typically pass the bar. You're stronger than average in school deciles, which helped balance things somewhat, but the model weighed your test scores and background as subtle risks relative to high performers. To improve your future chances, focus on what you can control: retaking the LSAT to aim above 36.7, building stronger study habits shown to boost bar passage like those behind top deciles, or gaining practical experience that bolsters GPA-like metrics in grad programs. With targeted bar prep courses emphasizing weak areas from your undergrad performance, many students in similar spots have turned predictions around. Keep pushing—you're already ahead in key academic deciles, and small improvements in testable skills could make a big difference next time. We're rooting for your success.
